comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What's the best way to break a habit that reduces willpower? I have a few I would like to stop, but I currently lack the willpower to do it. I know I should target them one at a time, but it really seems like a can in the can opener situation...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ignore the habits for the time being; fighting them just divides your will further. Choose something in which you have no emotional energy invested at all, and use that as a place to exercise your will and build its strength. Then apply the same logic to other parts of your life where you have no emotional energy invested, and start using them as opportunities for training the will.

Don't get into a fight between will and emotional energy any sooner than you need to -- that way lies failure. Once you've developed the habit of will in other areas of your life, you can assess the habits, decide on what changes you want to make, and make them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thinking about an example of this technique, would it be more like a) concentration practice, as in Sadhu's book Concentration (next on reading list, thank you for pointing it out!), Or b) like choosing dinner in the morning and then go ahead and cook it in the evening?

Or perhaps the are better examples entirely?

Sincerely, Svea

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can use either approach, or others. One very useful approach is to choose to do certain things for no other reason than to train your will. Find a different route to work from your usual, and take that route every day for the next week. Learn everything you can over the next month about the history of Afghanistan, without looking it up on the internet. It you wear a necktie, learn a different knot for it and start using that instead of the one you habitually use. Decide, for no reason at all, that at one o'clock in the afternoon next Saturday you are going to be at a particular place twenty miles away from your home -- a place where you have never been -- and then be there exactly on time. Change your habits, challenge your tastes, shake up your routines -- that's a very effective way to build strength of will.

replies.content: It's actually fairly common. Yawning is the reset button for your etheric body, and when you move energy up or down the centerline of your body, that can run into blockages -- the yawn is the process by which your body works at clearing those away.

replies.title: Re: Yawning

It is a variation on chakra and tree of life meditations many people are familiar with. The first steps involve sending a grounding cord from the solar plexus to the center of the earth, drawing energy up into the body, and circulating it by way of the spine. Then one opens the seventh chakra at the top of the skull, draws a smaller amount of cosmic energy down, and circulates that energy down the spine, around, and out. Every time I do this meditation, I yawn once or twice when I'm a couple of minutes into it. It's completely involuntary.

replies.title: Re: Discernment of spirits
replies.content:
I'm glad this topic came up, and hope we can return to it on a Monday in the near future. The milieu in which I grew up did not acknowledge psychic phenomena at all. I had to wait until I was a young adult before I met people who would give me some guidance in developing my modest psychic talents. By my choice, most of this teaching and practice has been in the context of wiccanate witchcraft.

There is reason to think that some groups that were precursors to what's now called Wicca worked with spirits routinely, but it looks as if transmission of this particular aspect of witchcraft was interrupted during or after Gardner's lifetime. Most Wiccans have some commerce with dead people whom they have ties with, but apart from that, interaction with spirits isn't practiced or discussed very much in a lot of wiccanate covens. This is quite different from what folk witches are reputed to do.

The tradition that gave me authority to initiate has circle casting procedures and methods of invocation that are effective filters through which only a few kinds of spirits are able to pass. That is useful for preparing a safe space in which to commune with deities and do certain kinds of magic.

But outside a cast circle, the world swarms with spirits. Most of which I just tune out. This is not very helpful when one has chosen a spiritual path that is largely about liminality. I would like to be better at discernment of spirits, both in the sense of noticing their presence, and in the sense of sizing up what sort of spirit they are.

replies.title: Re: Discernment of spirits
replies.content:
Some groups of pre-Gardnerian Witches in the USA came out of Spiritualism, and built their Circles with Spiritualist (largely, séance) practices. Ideally, such a Spiritualist Circle has precisely 13 members (half men, half women, plus a Medium as leader), work in a circle, raise power which is contained in their Circle, summon and dismiss spirits, and so forth. Since the Civil War there was also a push among Spiritualists to reclaim the word "Witch" as an empowering label for themselves and their powers.

My best example of this sort of Witchcraft may be the group that included Marion Koclo's women ancestors in northern Michigan. (She was better known as "Gundella, the Green Witch," in her day.) I have wondered whether the "Harpy Coven" that Victor Anderson knew in his young years was another such pre-Gardnerian group.

There were at least two, probably three other sorts of pre-Gardnerian Witches in the USA back in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, but they were Solitaries and did not use Circles or form "Covens" (by whatever name known). Some came out of New Thought, and others out of First Wave Feminism (under the influence of Matilda Joslyn Gage, who in turn was influenced by early Theosophy). Last of all came a few Feminist women on the West Coast who built their solitary practices out of semi-scholarly authors on Witchcraft such as Oliver Hueffer, Ian Ferguson, J. W. Wickwar, Theda Kenyon and Emile Grillot de Givry. (Some of these authors, especially Grillot de Givry, drew heavily on grimoire magic in their books, too.)

All of these Witches cross-fertilized with one another, and with Wicca, too, once it crossed the Atlantic to the USA (in the SF Bay Area, at least, that was a couple of years before Ray Buckland arrived on our shores).

I should also note that Gardner himself says in his autobiography (written for him by Idries Shah and published as if by Jack Bracelin) that he spent a lot of time looking into Spiritualism before he encountered the New Forest "Coven."

replies.title: Re: Dad
replies.content: Back in the day, Spiritualists faced this challenge with almost every seance, and wrestled with it hard.  Their conclusion: there's no way whatever for a Spiritualist to be quite sure.  One test that strikes me as better than most: Does contact with the supposed spirit bring you anything really beneficial way down the road for you and your family, as opposed to good feelings and a sense of closure?  In this realm, spirits are *very* often not whom they seem to be.

replies.title: Re: Dad
replies.content: Right. that’s why I’m not going to talk to it unless I’m pretty sure what it is.

I am guessing that mediation, prayer and contemplation of nature would be better choices for a person with a heart condition.

replies.title: Re: Heart Condition
replies.content: It's a traditional caution in occult studies that people who have heard conditions should avoid practicing ceremonial magic, other than such very basic techniques as the Sphere of Protection. Meditation, prayer, and contemplation of nature are entirely safe; an occasional Sphere of Protection would be okay, and so is celebrating the solstices and equinoxes -- but the Grail Working and other energy work, no, not until and unless she gets a clean bill of health from her health care provider.

comments.title: geomantic magic squares
comments.content:
The geomantic figures could be interpreted as sixteen binary numbers from 0 to 15. It would thus be possible to put them into any of the 880 possible 4x4 Magic Squares. For the compulsive, there is a blog post by digitalambler and some thoughts of my own on my blog, here;
http://jackmanassas.blogspot.com/2019/03/geomantic-magic-squares.html

Historically, has anyone or does anyone put geomantic figures into magic squares? If so, what could one do with such combinations?

Thanks in advance for any insights you can give on this one!
E. G.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content:
Seriously cool!

I don't know of anyone who's done anything like this, but magic squares are an important tool in several branches of ceremonial magic. You can place them on talismans, use them to generate sigils, and develop them into tablets for scrying, just for starters.

The crucial step in doing this would be to identify four of those 880 4x4 squares that are specially appropriate, one to each of the four elements. Once you've done that, you've got four geomantic tablets and a great deal of magic can be developed from them.

How to choose them? That's what I'm not sure of. Presumably the squares assigned to each element would have the figures assigned to that element in some unusually prominent place, but that's as far as I've gotten.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: I think I will try a geomantic reading to see if I get an answer to that.  It is certainly very interesting, at least to me!

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: Ok, I guess I am being particularly dense. I can see how Populus equals 0, but if one dot equals one, how do you get Via to equal 15, I come up with 4. What have I missed here?

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content:
Via = 1 fire + 1 air + 1 water + 1 earth.
If we treat these as powers of 2...
Via = 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^1 + 1
Via = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1
Via = 15
To get the other values, treat the 2 pip results as 0 in the appropriate spot.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: Thanks, that helps.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content:
It's being read as a binary number. Like in our usual (decimal, with 10 digits) number system 1111 means 1+10+100+1000, in a binary system 1111 means 1+2+4+8; if there are 10 digits, each position to the left tracks values 10 times larger, while for 2 digits each position is a doubling. So 1 is 1 in either system, binary 10 is decimal 2, binary 100 is decimal 4, binary 1011 is decimal 1+2+0+8=11, and so on.

So other such systems can exist for every number. In octal 17 means 8+7=15 in decimal, because the 8 digits are 0-7, so decimal 8 is written 10; 100 and 1000 would mean 8*8=64 and 8*8*8=512 respectively. Hexadecimal can be written using the letters A-F for what are in decimal 11-15, so decimal 15 is hexadecimal F and decimal 16 is hexadecimal 10.

Binary, which one can see in geomancy and the I Ching, also happens to be what computers use, because "no substantial electrical current" can be read as 0 and "an adequate electrical current" as 1, any number can be encoded binarily, and all manner of information can be encoded as numbers.

(I'd have just pointed to Wikipedia on binary numbers, except I didn't like it. Feedback on any shortcomings of this explanation welcome. - S. T. Silva.)

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: Thanks.  I used to know binary and hex at one time, but I seem to have forgotten it.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: I remember having this stuff in New Math in the 5th grade.  Your explanation is a lot better than theirs.

replies.title: Re: geomantic magic squares
replies.content: Unless you're in IT or a math teacher this is probably long forgotten (was for me!). Just remember any base is the same process. In decimal (base 10), you don't have to think about it. Right to left starting with the "units place" is 1s (10^0), 10s (10^1), 100s (10^2) - the little ^ thing stands for exponent or "to the power of". So in base 2 the units place is 2^0, etc. Hope that helps? If not there are lots of videos and other explanations on the net.

replies.content: Thanks much.  I have forgotten much of the math I ever knew.

comments.title: Druid grove and 1 year old
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Where, on the scale of magical safety around children in the FAQ, does the Druid Grove Ceremony (described in the Druidry Handbook and Druid Magic Handbook) fit? Does it change much with the inclusion of the SOP?

I feel like it would be safer than the SOP on it's own as the closing of the grove involves "cleaning up" the remaining energies. Would this make it OK in a space my 1 year old child will be spending their time, although performed when they're not there?

thanks (and I hope it's not to annoying to clarify this one)

replies.title: Re: Druid grove and 1 year old
replies.content: No, you're fine. The Druid Grove ceremony was designed and repeatedly reworked to be safe around basically anyone -- it's been used in plenty of public rituals, for example, and to my knowledge there has never once been a problem with it. The version in The Druidry Handbook, without the Sphere of Protection, is the one to use until your child is 3 or so.

replies.title: Re: Druid grove and 1 year old
replies.content:
Speaking of which, if you're an AODA druid doing, say, an Alban Eiler celebration, how should that fit in with your daily practice? Should you still do your SoP, meditation, and divination in the morning (if that's when you do them), and then do the larger ceremony on its own later in the day? Does the meditation that's part of the ceremony "count" for your daily meditation, or should you do another one at a later time?

Sorry if that's an odd question. This may be entirely a relic of something I learned as a Catholic, but I seem to have gotten the impression that you should avoid doing "too much" ritual in a day as well as too little.

replies.title: Re: Druid grove and 1 year old
replies.content: You can do either one. If anyone else is joining you in the seasonal celebrations, it makes sense to do your own practice in the morning and then do the seasonal celebration later; if you're doing it all by yourself, you could as well simply do the seasonal celebration as your morning practice.

Hi JMG,

I would appreciate your perspective on something I've witnessed. I have long known by way of mystical experience that the divine, the Mystery, the Big Wow, is beyond the human concept of "good" and "bad," but I think I still trip over the western notion of progress here and there.

I have lately had to realize that perhaps humans will choose a left hand path for their spiritual development--crossing the river Lethe of course, on the way to walking that path. I do not understand it. It makes me very sad and I try to carefully fulfill the bodhisattva vow to help all sentient beings (an incredibly perilous task) to the best of my abilities. But it seems like helping sometimes means just witnessing people wreck their lives and "descend" into a very difficult spiritual morass. I must poke at the language of ascent/descent here because I am starting to question how I hold that idea.

I've been made to notice that it is actually NOT helpful to a particular person, to ask other spiritual beings to intercede on their behalf. For example, when fending off their demon-assisted attacks, I would ask my protector beings to please help those demons back to their rightful place in the cosmos, that they be free from slavery and so on. I found it really taxed my own energy to make these requests. At some point, and without rancor, I asked that all the nasty be sent right back to whoever sent it to me...and I got the very distinct sense (mystical and intuitive) that that was the Good thing to do. That it was in accord. And to try to intervene in this person's "descent" into madness was actually NOT helping them. I am tearful in writing this. I am so sad for this person, despite the utterly ridiculous $*#)($* I have had to put up with for two years. They are suffering, too. I do not wish them more harm...and yet.

What perspective might you offer to this? This very personal experience has helped me to feel more confident in a larger orientation towards not demonizing anyone on the political spectrum, and truly seeing the sacred in all things--a challenging if not repulsive task at times, and not a path for the integrity-challenged.

p.s. - Also in follow up to my post on "Really big demons" last week - thank you to Anonymous for pointing me to Clifford Hartleigh Low's presentation on Fearless Magical Protection (here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GJ0j_ZJVxj4). with gratitude and the hope that all beings may benefit

replies.content:
Okay, good. One of the traps in the Bodhisattva practice is thinking you know what kind of help all sentient beings need. The cycle of incarnation, if I may fall back for a moment on the language of the Shingon Buddhism I encountered in my youth, is a cycle of pointless suffering only when seen from within the perspective of samsara; from the perspective of Nirvana, it is the expression of the infinite bliss of Mahavairocana, the source and root of all the Buddhas; and from the perspective between these two, the perspective of the bodhisattvas, it is the working out of infinite compassion through infinitely skillful means.

What that means in practice is that plunging straight into Hell may be the fastest and most certain way for a given sentient being to work through the burden of ignorance and karma, and get onto the path that leads to enlightenment. My sense has always been that judgment belongs to the gods, not to human beings, and that includes such judgments as "it's a cry for help" and "they're really not a bad person down under it all." I simply ask the gods I invoke to deal with a nasty person as they see fit, from the perspective of their divine knowledge, and leave it at that.

Speculatively: there are two factors. One, Chiron is not just a dwarf planet but a trapped comet, the first such body discovered in our solar system (though not the last). Additionally, it has an odd orbit which crosses back and forth between the orbit of Saturn and that of Uranus. People often feel that unusual things ought to have special significance.

Two, Chiron's myth has extra resonance for culturally Christian astrologers, because he trades his life for Prometheus' freedom. I've noticed that Christian cultures have a special affection for altruistic-martyrdom (Jesus) stories, the way Buddhist cultures are attracted to altruistic-renunciation (Siddhartha/Bodhisattva) stories (see their respective pop cultures for examples.)

That depends entirely on the details you haven't included -- and no, this is not an invitation to include them; doing even a halfway decent delineation of a natal chart takes hours of concentration and hard work.

To my mind, one of the common flaws in the modern approach to astrology is this idea that you can take one factor (say, the rough arrangement of the planets around the chart) and draw conclusions from that, irrespective of the rest of the chart. An astrological chart is a whole system, and has to be interpreted as such; doing anything else is like asking someone to explain the meaning of a paragraph when all you tell them is that the paragraph contains the letter E thirty-one times.

comments.title: Demons

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content: According to occult tradition, they are beings from a universe before ours, who could not (or would not) join with the other beings of that universe in its destiny at the end of that cycle of time, and remained behind, blind gnawing presences in the void. Certain events early in the history of our species brought us into contact with these beings. They regard us with hatred, jealousy and hunger, and they're perfectly willing to tell us anything we want to hear, in order to feed on us. They're one of the reasons that regular practice of banishing rituals is so important.

comments.title: Verbal thinking.
comments.content:
I think in part because of childhood and adolescent medications and partly so many years of meditation conditioning to keep an empty mind I feel that to actively think thoughts can act like weight living, heavy as if I am trying to drag out their shapes from some ultra thick soup.

I'd really like to form the habit of assessing situations, the variables or elements involved, weigh them in my mind, and create plans of action that align with the context, my motivations and desires. The main problem is this overwhelming urge to be passive and 'blank' instead, as if an overwhelming lethargy keeps me down like the depths of a briny ocean.

I can fight it at any given point, but the resistance I could manifest would often turn out as chaotic tremors, a powerful but seizing desire to yell and thrash about, unstable and easily over-heating of the chronic pressure layers. Mostly I just try to be patient bide my time until more favorable but uncommon moods spring forth for greater malleability and upward motivation to increase clarity in the subtle workings of how I can more efficiently hold myself to free up more resources for future-me.

Do you have any ideas of what might be blocking me the most and what I can do to make conscious cognition easier beyond habitual root practice of it?

replies.title: Re: Verbal thinking.
replies.content: The combination of drugs and thoughtstopping meditation can do a lot of damage to a growing mind, and it may take you many years to overcome it. Take it a little at a time; assign yourself a few minutes each day to think verbally, as clearly as possible, and let those few minutes seed the process. Over time, it'll become easier.

comments.title: LBRP, spirits, and Dion Fortune
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

First of all, thank you for your advice last week about incorporating a banishing ritual into my practice of the Experience of the Inner Worlds system. I experimented the next day with performing an LBRP before my other practices and the difference was so dramatically positive that it made me wonder if Knight assumed that the audience for his book would already be performing regular banishings as a matter of course. Not only did I feel more energized and centered generally, but the EOIW visualizations also felt more clear and stable. I'm going to make it a permanent part of my practice.

A couple of related questions. First, practical: should the second Q-cross be performed before I do the EOIW exercises, or at the end of the whole process?

Second, theoretical: in Circles of Power, you describe the Q-cross as a symbolic identification of the magician with Adam Qadmon, the image of God in which mankind is shaped. Why then is the ritual performed as though facing the Tree of Life (Geburah on right shoulder) rather than merged with it (Geburah on left)?

On a different note, I've been on something of a Dion Fortune binge lately, reading through Dr. Taverner and Psychic Self-Defense in tandem. These have also sparked questions. First, Fortune emphatically warns against calling on the beneficial powers of the spheres before having controlled the "demonic" side. I'm guessing that you disagree given your instructions for calling on the planetary intelligences and spirits in The Art and Science of Geomancy. Is Fortune just hoping to scare off dabblers, or is there something to what she's saying?

Finally, in Fortune's stories Dr. Taverner is frequently called upon to thwart the Black Lodges. Is this purely a literary device or is it actually common for occult orders to clash over matters of magical ethics?

Thanks again for generously fielding our many questions!

Sincerely, Fred N

replies.title: Re: LBRP, spirits, and Dion Fortune
replies.content:
Delighted to hear it.

1) Do the second Cabalistic Cross right before you do the EOTW exercises. Then, when you've finished the exercises, stand up and do a third.

2) Remember that Adam Cadmon is the reflection of God, not God. Just as a mirror reverses the sides of the image reflected in it, so the orientation of the energy centers in you mirrors, with sides reversed, the orientation of the energy centers in the body of God.

3) The planets are not the Spheres. The Spheres of the Tree of Life are the primary spiritual powers; the planets are their reflections in space and time. Thus it's safe, under appropriate conditions, to work with the planetary powers, where it's much more risky to work with the Spheres directly.

4) It's not that common these days, but there was a fair amount of it at some points in the past -- not so much about ethics, but ethics make a great rhetorical cover for struggles having to do with power and wealth. (Cough, cough, current US politics, cough, cough.)

replies.title: Re: LBRP, spirits, and Dion Fortune
replies.content:
Thanks for the clarification! I had a hunch that the answer to #2 would have to do with the reflection of the Divine image.

With regards to #4, your answer makes sense but opens some other very intriguing questions. Fortune paints the Black Lodges as solely interested in the use of magic to gain advantage over others, but your answer makes me think of a point you've raised before about magic being the weapon of the oppressed. Are you perhaps suggesting that Fortune's depiction is from the perspective of the elite, criticizing the use of magic by those who lacked more mundane means of securing an advantage in life?

replies.title: Re: LBRP, spirits, and Dion Fortune
replies.content: I think it was more a matter of political affiliations in the declining years of the British Empire. There were occult groups that wanted to see Ireland and India independent, and others that wanted to see the British Empire keep control of the lands it had seized; Fortune was rather in the latter camp. There's a section in her book Psychic Self-Defence in which you get to see a little of that.

replies.title: Re: Training The Will
replies.content:
Eating is less important than most people these days like to think. Certainly you need adequate calories, other necessary nutrients, etc., but you can get those in many different ways -- including ways that are officially "bad" according to this or that diet guru -- and you'll be just fine.

Sleep is much more important, and so is ordinary rest. The ability to sit back, let go, and not fret about anything for a while is immensely restorative to the will. You don't "actively" renew the will; you rest from action -- and that means letting go for a little while of all the desires and cravings and motives that keep your will hopping -- and it replenishes.

replies.title: Re: Lucifer
replies.content: Roman Catholic here:  make your offering to benefit the souls in Purgatory.  She’ll be pleased, especially if she’s one of them!

replies.title: Re: Lucifer
replies.content:
"I would like to do some kind of offering to an aunt who was good to me who died in the 80s. She was a devout Catholic but I’m not. Any suggestions? Also if she has been reincarnated in the meantime is there still anyone to do the offering to?"

As someone who is in a similar situation, my experience is that Catholic ancestors understand and welcome loving gestures, but prefer those gestures to take a familiar shape. In life they may have honoured their own ancestors by doing things like laying flowers on graves, lighting a commemorative candle, saying Hail Marys, or observing the All Souls Day customs of their culture, so those tend to please them. They usually also understand symbolic 'hospitality' like their favourite foods and drinks - the same sorts of things you would do if they visited your house while alive. However, performing unfamiliar ceremonies on their behalf may get a fretful "this isn't how they did All Souls Day in my day!"

I've never known a problem with reaching out to ancestors from much longer ago. I don't think you're necessarily reaching them 'now' but rather across time, in the outside-time space where the not-currently-incarnated go.

(As for Lucifer - lots of different beings use that name, and some pest spirits will introduce themselves that way just to get a big reaction. This is why old pagan invocations often started with "Hail, O great X, who is [long description of their ancestry, legendary deeds, and good qualities]" - not to flatter them, but to be very clear and specific about which X you meant!)

replies.title: Re: Lucifer
replies.content:
A suggestion, if it is OK: a very traditional offering for a devout Catholic is to have a Mass said for the person who died. This is understood to help them finish dealing with the issues (in Catholic language, "the temporal punishment for sin") remaining from their life.

Masses can be said at any time, but it's most traditional to ask for Masses to be said on days important to the person--anniversary of their death, their birthday, and so forth. Any Catholic church can accept a Mass intention for any Catholic who has died; the typical requested donation is in the $10 - $50 range at most US parishes.

SamChevre

comments.title: Raspberry Jam Principle
comments.content:
JMG-

I was reading on your other blog about the raspberry jam principle. It makes sense to me that when, for example, you curse someone you end up with a little bit of curse on yourself. However, why can't you just follow up that working with standard methods to purify yourself of negative energy and as a result avoid the fallout?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Raspberry Jam Principle
replies.content:
Because it's not just energy. Everything you do changes who you are; every one of your actions makes it more likely that you will do the same thing again, and less likely that you'll do the opposite; actions become habits, habits become character, and character becomes destiny.

For example, every time you use magic to deliberately hurt someone, you turn a little more into the kind of person who deliberately hurts other people; hurting other people comes to mind a little more often when you consider your options, and doing anything less nasty comes to mind a little less often; and since other people respond to the "vibe" you give off as well as your actions themselves, everyone else will be a little more likely to treat you as someone to avoid -- or to hurt. Rinse and repeat, and you've got the social psychology of the practitioner of nasty magic, isolated, shunned, hated and despised, hating and despising everyone in turn, and stewing in a lifelong vat of self-generated misery. Banishing rituals won't prevent that.

replies.title: Isaac Bonewits
replies.content:
I think that Isaac (I knew him well enough when we were both young to first-name him) made at least one lasting contribution to the world of alternative religions, the Advanced Bonewits Cult Danger Evaluation Frame, or ABCDEF for short. This was as far as I know an original piece of work.

It is a list of observable characteristics that mark an organization as a cult in the pejorative sense of that word. The list includes features that academics and law enforcement have pointed out as cult markers, but also things like humorlessness. I recommend anyone look up the ABCDEF with Bonewits' name on the Internet. If you run across a group or organization that looks interesting or attractive, but it scores high on the ABCDEF, you will save yourself a lot of trouble by not getting involved with it. Or if you are already involved, get out.

replies.title: Re: Guardian angel
replies.content:
Different traditions of occult philosophy have different views here. According to the account I find most convincing, yes, guardian angels are souls that have passed through the human stage of existence and now guide and protect souls still in human bodies as part of their own spiritual work.

You'll find other teachings that follow something closer to the Christian tradition, which is that angels are a separate order of being, distinct from human souls; that guardian angels (along with many other types of angels) belong to the lowest of the nine angelic orders; and that they are tasked with guidance and protection of human souls not for their spiritual advancement -- in this view, angels don't advance; each one has its proper place in the cosmos, which it fills joyously -- but because the overflowing love of the Divine flows through them and motivates them to acts of mercy.

Who's right? Good question. In practical terms, it comes to the same thing anyway.

replies.title: Re: Egregores of diseases
replies.content:
You may want to look into Homeopathy.

The gist of it is that there are Specific Remedies (those that have affinity for just one organ, or one type of tissue) and treat mostly diseases affecting those; and then there are Polycrest Remedies.

Polycrests on the other hand show symptomps all over the body, and treat wide ranges of diseases. More to the point, all these remedies are related with mental symptoms (sometimes severe psyquiatric ones, both more often than not mere neurotic responses to anxiety, or even personality quirks). Homeopathic Disease Theory teaches that physical disease that has established deep enough in the patient will manifest itself at least partially as a mental condition, while the same disease that attacks another person with stronger constitution will manifest exclusively on a material level (probably in an relatively periferic organ or system).

More over, there are Polycrests that have full-fledged Homeopathic Personalities, while others have mere collections of mental symptoms. Under the theory that individual diseases do form egregoires, this piece of lore supports the idea that some of these egregoires are stronger and/or more mature than others.

You may want to explore your individual case with a local, licenced Homeopath Doctor. Though if you expect to talk about the more occult side of the art you should stay clear of practicioners that follow the Frech school and try to find some classic/Kentian one instead.

CRP

replies.title: Re: Egregores of diseases
replies.content: I'd be interested in your take on Schuessler's biochemic cell salts, and the idiosyncratic but effective offshoot of homeopathy that uses that. That's the system that was adopted most enthusiastically into American occult schools, and the one I learned to practice.

replies.title: Re: Egregores of diseases
replies.content:
I know very little of the Schuessler salts. Both the claim that the salts "provide minerals that are normal components of the human body, and the patient is probably lacking in them" and the fact that these are prepared in fairly low potencies (parts per million) sound suspiciously close to the French materialist school to me, but then I would not have guessed these were specially popular in the occult scene.

One possible place to address this question is to compare Schuessler's writings to the teachings of Emmanuel Swedenborg. Many prominent homeopaths from the 19th century (Kent most prominently) were Swedenborgeans, and his philosophy seam to have shaped their own theories of disease. I am curious and would be glad to tackle this myself, somewhere down the road, but I do lack the bandwidth in the forseable future. It depends on whether I can find a sympathethic teacher and make this my disertation subject.

CRP

replies.title: Re: Understood
replies.content:
As for coming from a family to which one never belonged:

My own take on that question has simply been to learn from the traumas in one's life, and from the larger ones in proportion. I have a tea mug that says, "Scars are just tattoos with better stories." (Yes, I am a grim old man. I have had a grim outlook on life as far back as I can remember. I may even have been a grim baby when I was in my cradle ... 😛)

IMHO, one cannot *be healed* from one's traumas. One can use one's traumas as sources of strength. One can use them to give oneself a different view of life than the views other people normally have. One can work around them, much as armless people can develops skills and specific flexibilities in their legs that ordinary people will never know, or people blind from birth develop perceptions of their surroundings that sighted people can safely ignore. This is actuslly a "healing" of a sort.

Sometimes the answer to that question is simply: one was born into such a family simply so that one could never ever experience what it is to be a conventionally normal person, a person always and everywhere who fits in smoothly to the world.

There's actually a great deal to be said for living one's life askew from the world, for never being able to "fit in." The things one least wants in one's life, that have hurt one the most, that are still sources of pain, are often the very things that can be one's best sources of strength and wisdom.

Muriel Rukeyser once wrote "The universe is made of stories, not of atoms." There's a great deal of power in that out-of-step truth. Craft and tell yourself the right stories about your life and you can do things no one else can do. On occasion, you may even discover that the stories you can tell yourself about your life will be what you need to deal with some trauma of yours solely by your own efforts.

replies.title: Re: Understood
replies.content:
Some people have indeed been trained from childhood by their families and their age-mates to display such a sign on their foreheads. The sign itself is invisible to others, but as long as you feel it there, your unconscious body language will yell to the world at large, "I've got an abuse-me sign written in bright burning fire on my forehead! Act on it!" And the world will get the message, and mindlessly obey it.

So ... if it feels to you like it might be a good idea ... here's a simple and safe thing you could try for getting rid of that sign on your forehead.

There are a few very old symbols that proclaim, "I am protected powerfully from all abuse. This is not a threat I am making, just a simple fact of my being that everyone ought to know."

You could paint one of these symbols on your forehead with your finger dipped in ordinary tap-water every morning before you go out into the world, and you could also renew it privately--say, in a restroom--whenever it feels like it needs reinforcing. The water will slowly begin to dampen your fiery abuse-me sign; eventually it will put out the fire altogether.

My ancestry is Scandinavian, so I've used old Scandinavian protective symbols. The simplest such symbol is the old runic letter Algiz. It looks like our capital letter Y, but with the vertical line extended upward through the fork, like this: ᛉ . (If my image doesn't get through dreamwidth's font filter, look up the name "algiz" on wikipedia.)

Another such symbol, somewhat more complicated, consists of eight of these runic letters arranged like eight evenly-spaced spokes of a wheel. Their bottoms meet at the center of that wheel, and their forked ends are at its rim. Cross each of the eight spokes with three short lines between its fork and the center of the wheel. This is called the "Helm of Awe." For an image, look its name up on wikipedia. There's a picture there, too, though in that picture the forks are curved.

If those symbols are too Scandinavian or too pagan for your taste, you can get the same result with the Jewish six-pointed star, known as the "Shield of David." Or you can use any of the more elaborate Christian crosses, for example, the "cross pattée." (Again, see wikipedia for images.)

For a little extra whammy, put a bit of sea salt in the water that you use to paint the sign on your forehead in the morning. You don't need much salt at all for it to have some effect.

If you want a form of words to say over the water and salt as you mix them, you might use this one:

"Water from the earth, salt from the sea;
"Without them both I could not be;
"Both of them now strengthen me!"

Give it a try, if it feels right to you, for a few months. It usually works slowly, but it works surely.

comments.title: Rune Magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thank you for Magic Mondays! Each week I find new synchronicity while I'm combing through the comment section. There is always an answer to some question or other that has entered my mind during the intervening week. It never fails. However this go around I thought I'd ask one for myself...and maybe others.

replies.title: Re: Rune Magic
replies.content:
If I may... This sounds like it's maybe one of Edred Thorsson's books? I worked with one of them when I was first working with the runes several years ago. I found the runic poses useful for many months. However, when I began adding in more of his ideas, I would get a very heavy, dark feeling. I gave up my Taiji and qigong exercises while working with Thorsson's system. After maybe 15 months I had to quit: not only was the dark, heavy feeling growing, a chronic health issue was flaring up. I've since read on Reddit that some practitioners there consider Thorsson's system to be unbalanced.

After a few weeks away from his system, I began working with the 2 Heathen Golden Dawn rituals Mr. Greer and a collaborator are working on. That, and pitching Thorsson's book into the trash, got rid of the vestiges of his system. Due to other reasons, I am back with my qigong for energy work and my health issues are dissipating.

Meanwhile, following meditation, prayer and some rune casting, I have found that I can still do runic magic: mainly some basic galdr spells for protection. This seems to be working for me akin to natural magic. Mr. Greer may have a different opinion on whether or not this is natural magic.

Twice a year I take several weeks to review the rune poems and meditate on the runes via these, as well as some chanting of the rune names and maybe some vibration during the chanting. This helps with my discursive meditation on the rune poems.

I found the rune interpretations and meanings in Thorsson's book to be counterproductive. He has a penchant for giving facts solidly based on research then, without warning, throwing in his own interpretations and biases as if they were also from solid research. The 3 different rune poems are passably translated in Sweyn Plowright's "The Rune Primer". He had been a student of Thorsson's and talks about Thorsson and some other popular authors on the runes.

Another source I use is the Norwegian music group Wardruna. They've done 3 albums of songs based on the rune poems. The 2nd and 3rd have English translations of their songs with the albums. Neither their music nor their way of working with the runes is everybody's cup of tea. However, they worked with the rune poems via a lot of meditation over many years to come up with their songs.

Seriously, I would stay far away from the rune yoga. In retrospect, it harmed me in subtle ways.Wikipedia has an article on Guido von List, who first introduced the idea of runic yoga and was Thorsson's source for this. I learned a lot by reading about Thorsson at reddit via google searches, also.

I hope this helps.

DJSpo

replies.title: Re: Rune Magic

comments.title: Divination systems
comments.content:
I have used the I Ching with good results and am comfortable with it. Is there good reason to also try other systems like tarot, geomancy, astrology? Are there advantages to those other systems over the I Ching?

Thank you for your answers.

replies.title: Re: Divination systems
replies.content: If you get good results with the I Ching, stay with the I Ching. The only reasons you might choose to learn something else are (a) if you just happen to get curious, or (b) you decide to study a system of magic or esoteric spirituality that uses a different form of divination as a core element, in which case you do need to learn that.

replies.title: Re: Divination systems
replies.content:
The two systems of divination I rely on are Tarot and the I Ching. I would rate myself an intermediate practitioner of Tarot and no more than an advanced beginner with the I Ching. I've also dabbled in a different cartomancy system, the Lenormand deck.

What I have found is that at the introductory level, the kinds of questions that are easy to get clear answers on vary with different systems. One of the differences among systems is that some kinds of divination are oracular; they put you in communication with a spirit or intelligence which gives you information or advice. Other systems activate your own intuition and bring matters to your conscious attention that you didn't notice before. I see the I Ching as an example of the former and Tarot decks like the Waite-Smith deck being the latter. There are other types of divination such as taking of omens and pendulum work/dowsing which operate on other principles.

If you are well versed in Chinese culture and world view (which I am not), you meditate on the I Ching's symbolism and you consult the oracle regularly, you probably can ask it all sorts of questions and get good answers. My knowledge is more superficial, and I find that I get very clear advice mainly on matters dealing with organizations and interpersonal relations within them. When I was younger and asked questions on more personal matters, the answers weren't helpful to me because the outlook of the being I was consulting was too different from my own.

replies.content:

That makes sense about the I Ching being oracular, it does seem like speaking with a wise spirit. And I am more familiar with Chinese culture, ancient and modern, than most westerners. But what you say about the Tarot activating one's intuition is also interesting. I have approached that as a beginner in the past and may take it up more seriously if/when I have the time.

A couple weeks ago I asked about some Egyptian artifacts I had, and you prompted me to get them out and ask them what was up -- I have not yet done that, but only because I'm feeling so strongly about them I want to have a proper place and peace of mind to do so, and things have been busy here lately. Last Friday, however, I started doing the Polytheist Circle of Presence to Bastet, and from the first try it has left me with a lingering warmth around my heart, more than anything I felt in the three months I worked with the Welsh deities from the Celtic Golden Dawn. (I didn't get very far in CGD, just to the LRP daily practice, and have since kind of rebooted my practice -- as cleanly as I could -- with Learning Ritual Magic, which I'm finding is suiting me better in several respects. Now I have this God of Israel in the book/Egyptian Pantheon thing to work with, hence looking up the polytheist rituals here, but that's kind of another topic...)

Anyway, I've often seen commentary here about former Christians being mysteriously pulled back towards Christianity after encountering Golden Dawn/Ceremonial Magic, and I was almost one of those. After about twenty years of Buddhism and being off Christianity entirely -- readily saying things like, "It's just a 2,000 year old book" and being much more comfortable with the idea of a universal spirit of the cosmos -- I went back to my childhood church around Christmastime with my mother, and it all seemed very real, very potent, for the first time in a very long time. I had always tried to have a relationship with God as a child, as that's what I was taught (and my brother's relationship was so deep he went to seminary and became an ordained minister) but I had also always felt a deep relationship with nature and a lot of other just... "weird" things (including the teenage Egyptian phase.) At Christmas, however, I could feel Christianity as a kind of... I don't want to say "potential option", but a very valid practice, and for the first time in a very long time felt an authentic respect for my brother's experiences and beliefs. I haven't recited the prayers and all with the congregation in years, but I did sing the hymns, as I do like to sing. There was an energy and movement there I couldn't deny, but it still left me feeling fraught and drained afterwards, as going to church always had.

Then this Sunday, I had to take my mother to church again, since she can't drive due to a minor injury. That morning, I had done the Circle of Presence to Bastet for just the third time, kind of stumbling through it still, but still it left that kind of warm presence around my heart. As soon as I was settled in the pew at church and looking at the order of worship, I knew I could not -- should not -- speak or sing a word addressing the Christian God. Just looking at the words in the hymnal was like, "Nope." I did the sitting and standing to hold the book for my mother, but otherwise did not participate at all, and for years I've gone from time to time and still at least sung/taken communion (luckily communion wasn't served as I don't know what I would have done.) That lingering feeling around my heart...I kept feeling like I was wearing some kind of necklace or amulet -- like I had to remind myself I wasn't, the feeling was so present.

And when it was over, I didn't feel tired or angry or tense like I usually would after a church service. It was as if I'd just passed an hour's worth of time in some public space, very impersonal to me. The only metaphor that readily comes to mind is like when a new person shows up in your life who helps you get over an ex you had a bad breakup with. Like going to church was suddenly no big deal.

Which makes me very curious about your answer to the earlier question here about Christian baptism. I was baptized when I was an infant so didn't have much say in the matter. My brother has reminded me on occasion that once God knows your name He always knows your name, and "You might not believe in God, but God believes in YOU." I was never sure whether to take that as a comfort or a threat! Having been raised on Charlton Heston's The Ten Commandments, I'm more than a little worried? frightened? about nurturing a relationship with the Egyptian pantheon after being brought up Christian. But here's to hoping no wires cross-- unless that was some of the source of my discomfort all along.


Though I feel like I've finally entered a place where things are feeling "right" for me -- like welcoming, in a way -- I can definitely relate to that subtle pull back to the church. For a while, at least, it was a very tangible thing, like someone waving in a rear-view mirror. Now I just find myself angry that any alternative had been denied to me for so long.

Diane

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It sounds to me as though you've begun to establish a good working relationship with a deity who's right for you. As I noted in response to another commenter, from everything I've seen, it's not Jesus who's jealous, it's (some of) his followers. The Netjeru, the gods and goddesses of Egypt, are potent spiritual powers in their own right; I've worked with them in a trad Golden Dawn context and have experienced a little of their power, and you're not turning to a second-rate deity -- not by a long shot. I'd encourage you to continue developing your relationship with Bastet, and don't worry about Charlton Heston!

comments.title: How do you know vs believe?
comments.content:
Hi John, When you cite various occult things you generally come across as being absolutely *certain*. As we both know, there are a lot of religions in the world and every one I am aware of generally speaks as if the representative is absolutely certain, as well.

One reasons I respect you so much, and strive to remain open to idea that you are correct (no matter how far you depart from the objective world as I see it) is your logic. Another is your honesty in admitting when you don't know.

Anyway, here is my question: when you are explaining really abstruse stuff -- such as the meaning and validity of "The Cosmic Doctrine", how much are you simply trusting the author and how much are you validating via magical or discursive means?

That's probably a fairly broad question but it would be helpful to me, and probably to others, if you could expound on your means of certitude a little more.

Thanks,
gnat

replies.title: Re: How do you know vs believe?
replies.content:
Remember that I have Aspergers syndrome and therefore there are social cues in most people's writing and conversation that are not present in mine. This notion that I'm absolutely certain about what I'm saying is apparently an effect of that. I don't claim any such certainty; I am acutely aware of the limits of my knowledge, and of the limits of human knowledge generally -- but, my nervous system being what it is, I basically can't control the way other people (mis)interpret what I say.

To answer your question, though -- well, to begin with, did you notice that The Cosmic Doctrine is a set of metaphors? A metaphor can't be valid, it can only be useful. I've experienced the usefulness of the Cos. Doc. in many ways; I leave it up to each individual reader to consider the metaphor, test it against their own experience, and decide for themselves whether it's useful or not.

More broadly, I'm not into parroting the writings of authors whose work I haven't tested in various ways. At the same time -- and please understand the following words in their strict literal sense! -- I do not expect anyone to accept anything I say, just because I say it. My words are a map. You get to decide how well it fits the territory.

replies.title: Re: How do you know vs believe?
replies.content: The interesting thing is that I get the opposite impression: I feel like your writings give across a "Here's what I know works, but your mileage may vary, and I'm not sure of the truth of any of it."

replies.title: Re: How do you know vs believe?
replies.content: That's what I've always tried to communicate, but Gnat's far from the only person who somehow gets the opposite message.

replies.title: Re: How do you know vs believe?
replies.content: It occurs to me I'm autistic, so maybe your message comes across to people on the spectrum but gets lost for those who aren't.

replies.title: Re: How do you know vs believe?
replies.content: I also get that opposite impression, and I'm not on the autistic spectrum (or not diagnosed...).  I've been reading JMG for about 10 years, ever since I saw a reference in The Oil Drum.  What drew me to him is a sense of humility in what he knows, and so my acceptance of what is valid has broadened by reading this and his other writings.
